text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to expose the pelvis by lifting the rectum and sigmoid colon laterally. The left hand holds a fenestrated bipolar device, while the right hand uses scissors. The third robotic arm is positioned below the other instruments to assist in gaining optimal exposure.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to expose the pelvis by lifting the rectum and sigmoid colon laterally. The left hand holds a fenestrated bipolar device, while the right hand uses scissors. The third robotic arm is positioned underneath to assist in gaining optimal exposure.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum out of the pelvis, exposing the presacral space. The instrument is exchanged from below to avoid internal collision, and the rectum is lifted in a cephalad manner, preparing for the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to lift the rectum out of the pelvis in a cephalad manner, exposing the presacral space. The instrument is exchanged from below to avoid internal collision, with the tip oriented away from the surgeon.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by lifting the rectum in a cephalad manner. The third robotic arm is positioned to assist, while nerves are gently pushed down to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by lifting the rectum in a cephalad manner using robotic arms. The third arm is positioned to assist, while nerves are gently pushed down to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon achieves optimal exposure by lifting the rectum cephalad, initiating dissection in the posterior plane using a hook cadre to enter the areolar tissue plane. The tip-up grasper remains stationary during this phase of the operation.

The surgeon achieves optimal exposure by lifting the rectum cephalad, initiating dissection in the posterior plane using a hook cadre to enter the areolar tissue plane. The tip-up grasper remains stationary throughout this part of the operation.

The surgeon uses the fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left hand to provide macroscopic traction, functioning like a Parkes retractor. This instrument performs micro movements to create tension, aiding in the manipulation and exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left hand to provide macroscopic traction, acting like a Parkes retractor. The instrument performs micro movements to create tension, facilitating the exposure and manipulation of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the left hand to perform a three-dimensional scooping motion, pushing into the midline, lifting anteriorly, and pulling towards the head. This motion is applied starting at the posterior midline, maturing the dissection to the left and then to the right, continuing the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon uses the left hand to perform a three-dimensional scooping motion, pushing into the midline, lifting anteriorly, and pulling towards the head. This motion is applied to mature the dissection from the posterior midline to the left and then to the right, continuing the total mesorectal excision (TME).
The surgeon creates a hole in the posterior midline and works on expanding the defect to the left, carefully addressing small bleeders to avoid violating the mesorectal envelope.

The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to create a hole in the posterior midline, carefully expanding the defect towards the left while controlling small bleeders to preserve the integrity of the mesorectal envelope.

The surgeon creates a central defect in the pelvic region and matures it to the left and right. Visible bleeding occurs deep in the pelvis, near the levator plate and precyclovenous plexus. The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar cautery to manage the bleeding, avoiding cauterization directly on these structures to prevent damage.

The surgeon creates a central defect in the pelvic region and matures it to the left and right. The video shows bleeding in the deep pelvis, highlighting the importance of using a fenestrated bipolar cautery to manage bleeding without damaging the levator plate or precyclovenous plexus.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection by peeling off lateral attachments from the mesorectum, lifting it off the areolar tissue plane on the left side. The same technique is then applied to the right side, maintaining the mesorectum's elevation from the embryological plane.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, peeling off lateral attachments from the middle to the left, lifting the mesorectum off the areolar tissue plane. The same technique is applied to the right side, separating the mesorectum from the embryological plane.

The surgeon scores the anterior peritoneal reflection at the sulcus using hook cautery. A vaginal dilator, specifically an EEA sizer, is used to identify the anterior wall of the rectum and the posterior wall of the vagina. The surgeon then enters the rectovaginal septum with cautery. The assistant uses suction and pulls the recto-sigmoid junction out of the pelvis to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon scores the anterior peritoneal reflection at the sulcus using hook cautery, identifying the rectovaginal septum. The assistant uses suction and pulls the recto-sigmoid junction out of the pelvis to facilitate the operation.

The surgeon uses the assist to pull the rectum out of the pelvis, exposing the rectovaginal septum. With the third arm, the surgeon pokes, spreads, and lifts, dissecting the anterior wall of the rectum from the posterior wall of the vagina in an avascular plane. A defect is created midline, then matured to the left and right. The fellow takes over to address thin, filmy lateral stalks, unzipping them and dividing the last attachments down to the pelvic floor, ensuring circumferential dissection to the levator plate.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to poke, spread, and lift the rectovaginal septum, dissecting the anterior wall of the rectum from the posterior wall of the vagina in an avascular plane. A defect is created midline, then matured to the left and right. Thin, filmy lateral stocks are dissected, and the fellow completes the circumferential dissection down to the levator plate, ensuring full mobilization of the mesorectum.

The surgeon leaves a portion of the mesorectum intact due to the proximal location of the rectal cancer. If the cancer were located lower, the surgeon would staple across the rectum at the levator plate or distal to the tumor.

The surgeon leaves a portion of the mesorectum intact due to the proximal location of the rectal cancer. If the cancer were located lower, the surgeon would staple across the area near the levator plate or distal to the tumor.

The surgeon completes the division of the rectum using a robotic stapler with a green load, cutting at a right angle to the rectum. The stapler is positioned low in the pelvis, where a 30mm robotic staple is preferred for its maneuverability compared to larger sizes.

The surgeon completes the division of the rectum using a green load robotic stapler, applying a 30mm staple at a right angle to the rectum. The stapler is positioned low in the pelvis for optimal fit and articulation.

The surgeon tentatively pulls the rectum anteriorly, exposing the mesorectum and a visible line indicating nervous structures. Care is taken to avoid entering a false plane or the periosteal layer of the pre-sacral space. The surgeon uses cautery to separate aerial tissue and recreate the pre-sacral space, while the third arm remains stationary.

The surgeon tentatively pulls the rectum anteriorly, revealing the mesorectum and a visible line indicating nervous structures. Care is taken to avoid entering a false plane or the periosteal layer of the pre-sacral space. The surgeon uses cautery to separate aerial tissue and recreate the pre-sacral space, while the third robotic arm remains stationary.

The surgeon uses the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar to lift and push tissue, mimicking a Parkes detractor, while the right hand operates a hook cautery. The presacral space is exposed, with nerves tented up and manipulated to enter the true presacral space. The hypogastric nerve plexus is pulled down using the left hand, while the third arm remains stationary.

The surgeon uses the left hand with a fenestrated bipolar to manipulate tissues, while the right hand operates a hook cautery. The left hand acts as a right angle retractor, lifting and pushing tissues towards the head. The presacral space is visible, with nerves being tented up. The surgeon carefully pulls down on the hypogastric nerve plexus to enter the true presacral space. The third arm remains fixed in position.

The assistant uses suction to maintain a clear view as the surgeon matures the pre-circular space and approaches the TME plane. The surgeon creates contouring marks around the anterior peritoneal reflection to guide the incision, ensuring precise scoring at the peritoneal sulcus.

The surgeon matures the precircular space and approaches the TME plane. Using contouring marks, they score the anterior peritoneal reflection at the peritoneal sulcus, creating a trace mark for precise incision. An assistant holds down suction during the posterior dissection.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection with the third arm inserted at the anterior midline, spreading and lifting towards the head to fan the anterior peritoneal reflection. The assist uses suction to clear smoke plumes, improving visibility as the camera lens clears.

The surgeon uses the third arm to perform anterior dissection by inserting it at the anterior midline, spreading, and lifting towards the head to achieve fanning of the anterior peritoneal flexion. The camera lens is initially fogged, but clears as the assistant suctions plumes of smoke using high flow insufflation technology. The anterior retraction occurs as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to apply deep counter-traction on the left anterior attachments of the disorectum. The third arm is positioned in the left anterior area while the left hand pulls in the five o'clock direction, providing counter-traction against the levator plate.

The surgeon uses the third arm to provide deep counter-traction on the rectum, positioning it at eleven o'clock while the left hand pulls at five o'clock to detach the left anterior attachments. The levator plate is visible as the rectum is pulled down. For the right anterior attachments, the third arm is moved to two o'clock, and the left hand pulls at seven o'clock. The surgeon switches to a zero-degree scope for a direct view below the sacral promontory, optimizing visualization during the procedure.

The surgeon positions the third arm at the eleven o'clock position while the left hand pulls towards the five o'clock position to achieve counter traction. On the right anterior side, the third arm is moved to the two o'clock position, and the left hand pulls down to the seven o'clock position. The surgeon switches to a zero-degree scope for a direct view of the presacral space during the procedure on a patient with low-lying rectal cancer.
